Welcome back. In this lesson, we're going to talk about the negotiation process. Negotiations is probably one of the funnest things for me because it gives you an opportunity to find compromises where you can create mutual value. Remember, that's what you're supposed to do in the negotiation process. Find compromises to give mutual value. That's how you get deals done. Not try to get a one-up on somebody. Find compromises for mutual value creation. Now, let's start with the eight pillars of negotiation. Pillar number one, vision for the negotiation. You should not go into a conversation unless you're prepared to have it. The more you can structure compromising to a conclusion for a seller and for the buyer, listening, negotiating and concluding one step at a time to make it fair and equitable for everyone. And sometimes you have to plead your case. But setting up the vision for this, how the seller can win, what you appreciate about the business, and how you believe that by them selling to you, you can create value for the team members and for the customers that that business serves. Set the vision. Okay, now there's pillar two. Set the stage for the relationship. Don't go in with guns ablazing and trying to be a big wheel and trying to, oh, I got my line of credit, so now you're going to sell to me. Don't do any of that silly, stupid stuff that a lot of people quite honestly do. Go in with humility. Go in with appreciation. Go in understanding that this seller is selling something to you that could quite possibly be the single most important thing they've ever done in their life and respect that. Also set the stage for the relationship with the employees, the key employees. You're going to need them to help you grow that business. In addition to that, set the stage for the relationships with the vendors. You know what? Some of these businesses have been working with the same suppliers and the same vendors for dozens of years. And they're concerned about their supply relationships. Don't go in telling them you're going to change everything because now you're a bigger or better operator than they are. Go in with a little bit of humility to find that compromise within the new relationship. Pillar number three, connect using the three C's. Curiosity, clear communication, and choreograph pain points. Look, readdress why they were selling the business in the first place. Remind them why they entered into this with you. Remind them of what they wanted to get out of. But also communicate the value you're going to bring into it so they can feel like that baby they birthed is going to be well taken care of in the future. And be curious. If you find that they're uncertain, they're like you're getting information out of them as hard, ask good questions. Find out where they're sticking in the communication. See I like the three C's. Clear communication and choreograph where you want to go with that business owner. Now let's move to pillar four, quality through proper questions. See John Maxwell talks about law of curiosity. Sharon Lecter talks about intellectual curiosity. Why? Because the more curious you are, the better questions you ask, the better questions you ask, the better commentary that you can get so that you can get a compromise conclusion. I keep talking about compromise conclusion. The idea and the art of negotiations is to compromise on both sides so you can conclude something that will benefit both parties. That's the spirit in which you want to go into this conversation. So you want to qualify through proper questions the thought process of the seller so they can be at ease and be respectful of your thought process as the buyer. Next we'll talk about pillar five. Making negotiation needs based on identifying seller pain points. This is really important. See when I sit down with someone to talk to them I recount where we started. Mr. and Mrs. Seller, when we first started this conversation you had three specific things that you were most concerned about. You wanted to make sure that you got a fair price for your business. You wanted to make sure your key employees were taken care of. And you wanted to make sure that the same quality of care for your customers stays in place when and after you sell the business. I wanted you to know that all the way through this process I tracked on that. And here's some of the work I've done to ensure that when we conclude you'll have my commitment that we will do those things. You see I reminded them of their pain points and then I gave them a solution back to put them at ease. If you don't listen to people or you don't remind them of how they got there they may forget. So it's always important to remind people how you landed where you're at and the compromises and the work you've done to ensure their concerns are covered. Pillar number six, any modifications to the LOI should come with strong rationale after due diligence so the seller can see that you've done the work necessary. You're not trying to cram down on them. You're trying to work with them based on the facts that you've been able to see. Pillar number seven, pain points and how to navigate and work through them. Pillar number eight, pitch the offer to solve the seller's needs. Make sure you address how you've identified what the seller's priorities were and how you've structured the deal in order to accommodate the seller. Now if there are compromises that need to be made it's good to pull those out, go through them specifically. Don't bundle them together. I have to adjust the purchase rights because of this and this and this and this and this and this. Pull those elements out. Identify each element, what you identified, what your concern is and why you modified or need to modify the agreement so that you can break down the discussion to three to five key points not the whole deal all over again. You want to try to narrow and whittle down to the fewest things possible so you have a seamless close on the business. One other thing that you need to understand in pillar eight is that you also have to talk about post-closing expectations. What kind of support you're going to get, how they see themselves involved, your accessibility to them, any help you might need for 90 days on questions you have. You want to cover that off in pillar number eight. When you're solving the seller's needs you need to talk about them helping you solve your needs as well. That's it for the vetting and negotiation module. We'll see you in the next module.
